---
title: "Hidden Markov Models"
description: |
  Applications of Hidden Markov Models in Tracking the State of Health
format: 
  revealjs:
    footer: "Hidden Markov Models"
date: 2026-02-03
# image: Thomas_Bayes.gif
---


## Introduction

- Item A
- Item B


## States

::: {#fig-states layout-ncol=3}

![not_hungry](not_hungry.png)

![hungry](hungry.png)

![hangry](hangry.png)

Three discrete states of hunger.
:::


## State dynamics

```{python}
#| label: fig-states-unrolled-over-time
#| fig-cap: The states of hunger unrolled over time.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.offsetbox import OffsetImage, AnnotationBbox
from datetime import datetime

np.random.seed(datetime.now().year)
num_timesteps = 24
time = np.arange(num_timesteps)

state_emojis = ["./img/not_hungry.png", "./img/hungry.png", "./img/hangry.png"]
num_states = len(state_emojis)

image_boxes = {
    idx: OffsetImage(plt.imread(fname), zoom=0.4)
    for idx, fname in enumerate(state_emojis)
}

states = []
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 2))
# ax.plot(time, np.sin(2 * np.pi / 24 * time), lw=2, c="gray")
for idx in range(32):
    random_idx = np.random.randint(num_states)
    if idx <=12:
        ax.add_artist(
            AnnotationBbox(image_boxes[random_idx], (2 * idx, 0.5), frameon=False)
        )
    states.append(random_idx)
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(0, 24 + 2, 2))
ax.set_xticklabels([f"{t/2:.0f}" for t in np.arange(0, 24 + 2, 2)])
ax.set_ylim((0, 1))
# ax.grid(which="both", axis="both")
ax.spines[["top", "right", "left"]].set_visible(False)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_yticklabels([])
ax.set_xlabel("time")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

states = np.asarray(states)
```

## State transitions

```{python}
state_transition_matrix = np.zeros((num_states, num_states))
for idx in range(len(states) - 1):
    state_transition_matrix[states[idx], states[idx+1]] += 1
state_transition_matrix /= np.sum(state_transition_matrix, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
print(state_transition_matrix)
```
